artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

I’m hiding in my classroom for a second before dismissal, so this will be a little rambly.

I keep thinking about how much our work lives and family stuff have collided these last couple of years. Ever since Mom’s panic attack in the Riverside auditorium at Lucia’s 8th‑grade promotion on June 3, 2023, when the EMTs from Riverton Fire Station 4 had to come in front of everyone, I’ve been pushing to build “calm rooms” and movement breaks into big events. Kids still bring that night up. A few told me later that seeing her collapse made them scared to sit through assemblies at all.

Now I’m trying to use the same Mindful Movement idea everywhere. I’ve got a small group of 7th graders from Ms. O’Connor’s language arts class coming to Room 214 twice a week during advisory because of that lockdown drill incident on September 18, 2024, when one of them had a full‑on panic attack. I’ve been adapting grounding and breathing exercises from a clinician here, Dr. Priya Desai at Riverton Children’s Behavioral Health Center, so they’re short enough to fit into 10 minutes.

On the grown‑up side, I’m still guarding my own limits after the April 2024 ER trip to Riverton General when my chest pain turned out to be a panic attack from 60–70 hour weeks plus running Mom to chemo at Riverton Oncology Associates. My therapist, Dr. Karen Liu, has me on this 10‑minute “Stress Reset” routine that I’m now sneaking into our staff wellness challenge.

Anyway, how’s Columbus treating you? Still buried in help‑desk tickets? Come down some weekend so we can walk the river trail and not talk about panic attacks for once.

Love,
Sarah

9/25/24 – Mindful Movement / SEL notes

1. 7th-grade advisory pilot (Room 214)
- Group: students from Ms. O’Connor’s 7th-grade language arts class who were present during the 9/18/24 lockdown drill when one student had a panic attack.
- Schedule: Tuesdays/Thursdays during advisory in Room 214.
- Using Dr. Priya Desai’s grounding tools from Riverton Children’s Behavioral Health Center – today tried 4‑7‑8 breathing + simple chair yoga sequence.
- Goal: make these routines short enough that teachers can realistically use them (3–5 minutes) without feeling like it’s “one more thing.”

2. Staff modeling / my own limits
- Remember April 2024 ER visit at Riverton General Hospital (chest pain → panic attack). Trigger combo: 60–70 hour weeks during state testing + taking Mom to chemo at Riverton Oncology Associates.
- Dr. Karen Liu’s 10‑minute “Stress Reset” (box breathing, shoulder rolls, “name 5 things you see/hear/feel”) is non‑negotiable on testing days.
- Build this into next staff wellness challenge as an optional “Stress Reset” track so I’m not the only one using it.

3. Event planning
- Use Mom’s June 3, 2023 promotion‑night panic attack in the Riverside auditorium (EMTs from Riverton Fire Station 4 responding in front of the whole crowd) as a mental reminder: big events need quiet rooms and movement options.
- For documentation that goes public, keep examples general; no personal health details.

4. To‑do
- Draft one‑page Mindful Movement menu for teachers.
- Ask Dr. Desai if she’s comfortable with me sharing her adapted scripts.
- Block 10 minutes after dismissal for my own reset, especially on drill days.

Hi Karen,

I wanted to capture some of what’s been happening connected to wellness and supervision at our after‑school events, since it touches both student SEL and the broader “healthy activities” piece.

The 7th‑grade Halloween dance on October 27, 2024 in the gym was the big wake‑up call. When staff found vaping devices and what turned out to be prescription pills in the bathroom, we ended up calling Officer James Keller. Three students, including one of Lucia’s close friends, were transported to Riverton General Hospital as a precaution. Everyone is physically okay, but the kids are rattled and parents are understandably anxious.

This is on top of a string of other incidents that point to the same need for clearer expectations. In February 2024, Robotics advisor Mark Johnson let kids sell homemade tamales out of coolers in the back hallway near Room 214. One 7th grader with a severe shellfish allergy spent four hours in the Riverton General ER because the family who cooked them had prepped shrimp earlier that day. Then there was Culture Night on May 10, 2024, when a parent showed up visibly intoxicated and tried to hand out “adult” horchata in the parking lot. I ended up calling Daniel from Riverton City Public Works on his way home to help de‑escalate because her 6‑year‑old was sobbing and refusing to leave.

I’d like us to revise our guidelines so that: (1) supervision maps and SEL mini‑lessons are built into every big event, (2) any food‑based fundraiser is pre‑approved and follows food‑safety/allergy protocols, and (3) we state clearly that evening family events are alcohol‑free spaces.

Happy to draft language and bring it to our next leadership meeting.

Best,
Sarah

metadata.subject: After-school events: supervision, food, and wellness expectations

artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

I hope your week at Riverton Pediatric Behavioral Clinic is going as smoothly as possible.

I wanted to update you on how we’re trying to support Lucia at school using the coping tools you shared after her anxiety diagnosis on 10/12/2023. Since she started 6th grade at Riverside this fall, I’ve been coordinating with her homeroom teacher, Ms. Patel, to weave your grounding exercises into the 3–5 minute Mindful Movement breaks we’re piloting school‑wide.

Each Monday I email Ms. Patel a simple “Lucia Mindful Movement checklist” for the week. It includes 1–2 of the strategies you recommended (the “5 things you can see/hear/feel” grounding, the belly breathing with hands on the desk, etc.) plus one of the short chair stretches we’re using with other classes. The idea is that Lucia doesn’t feel singled out because the whole class participates, but we can still be intentional about the tools that match her plan.

On my end, I’ve asked our school health aide to help me track Lucia’s stomachache complaints and nurse visits in the health office log. We’re just noting dates/times and whether she used one of the grounding strategies first. So far this quarter she’s down to about one nurse visit every week or two, versus several times a week last spring.

I’m also doing similar work with a small 7th‑grade group in Room 214 on Tuesdays and Thursdays during advisory, using adapted breathing and grounding techniques from Dr. Priya Desai at Riverton Children’s Behavioral Health Center. It’s been helpful to see what lands with them as I think about which strategies are age‑appropriate for Lucia’s peers.

If you have any concerns about how we’re translating your recommendations into the classroom, or ideas for other low‑key supports, I’d really welcome your feedback.

With gratitude,
Sarah Martinez

metadata.subject: School implementation of Lucia’s coping strategies

artifacts.content:
Event: Coaching – A. Briggs (Mindful Movement/SEL)
Date: Wednesday, 2024-11-06
Time: 3:15–3:45 p.m.
Location: Riverside Middle School – Assistant Principal’s Office
Attendees: Sarah Martinez, Mr. Alan Briggs

Description/Notes:
Ongoing every other Wednesday since January 2024.

Purpose: Support Alan with concrete strategies to manage escalating moments in his 6th-grade classroom, especially given the performance improvement plan he’s been on since November 2023 after multiple parent complaints about yelling during last year’s spring testing.

Agenda for this session:
- Quick check-in about how last week’s techniques went (count-to-10 pause before responding; silent “reset” by placing both feet on the floor and taking three slow breaths instead of raising his voice).
- Practice a 3-minute breathing and stretching sequence he can use between classes or right after a tough interaction: shoulder rolls, neck stretches, inhale for 4/exhale for 6, brief grounding (“name three things you can see, two you can hear, one you can feel”).
- Role-play a testing scenario where noise or off-task behavior starts to spike, and have him choose from a menu of responses that keep him in the room and regulated.
- Add brief reflection to his private PIP log: What worked, what didn’t, any moments where he caught himself before yelling.

Reminders to self:
- Keep tone supportive, not punitive.
- Tie this back to staff wellness work (if teachers are dysregulated, students feel it).
- End on one specific commitment Alan will try before our next every-other-Wednesday check‑in.

3/5/24 – Staff wellness + absences

Pulled absence reports for Riverside + Oakview feeder staff from August 2023 to May 2024 (YTD). Noticed a worrying pattern: three paraprofessionals and one cafeteria worker from Oakview Elementary each have more than 18 stress-related absences documented. One of the paras met with me this afternoon in my AP office at Riverside and, in tears, shared that she’s now on an antidepressant prescribed at Riverton Community Mental Health after the workplace harassment complaint she filed back in October 2023.

This lines up with what I’ve been seeing in hallways – folks exhausted, snappy, calling off at the last minute. It also hits close to home after my own April 2024 trip to the Riverton General ER for chest pain that turned out to be a panic attack tied to 60–70 hour weeks during state testing plus juggling Mom’s chemo at Riverton Oncology Associates.

I need to fold this into our staff wellness challenges more intentionally. The step counts and hydration goals are fine, but they’re not touching the people who are barely making it to work. Thinking about adding a “Stress Reset” track based on the 10‑minute routine Dr. Karen Liu gave me (breathing, grounding, quick body scan) and offering optional small-group sessions before or after school.

Action items:
- Email HR director re: patterns in Oakview data and ask about existing supports.
- Talk with Oakview principal about whether they’d co‑host a joint wellness session.
- Build anonymous check‑in question into next staff meeting: “How many days in the last month have you considered calling in just from stress?”
- Protect my own boundaries so I don’t become the next 18‑absence case.

Hi Maya,

Catching my breath after tonight’s Family Wellness Night in the library and wanted to debrief while it’s fresh.

The turnout was good, and most of the stations went smoothly, but we had a curveball around the mindfulness piece. During the last rotation, I led a short guided meditation with Lucia’s group – just five minutes of breathing and noticing sensations, nothing overtly spiritual. Afterward, Pastor Lee from New Hope Baptist pulled me aside. He was very polite, but said a parent from his congregation felt the meditation conflicted with their religious beliefs and is considering talking to a lawyer at the Riverton Legal Aid Clinic.

It immediately brought back some of the other edge cases we’ve had this year: the tamale fundraiser in February 2024 when Mark Johnson’s Robotics Club kids were selling homemade tamales out of coolers by Room 214 and a 7th grader with a shellfish allergy ended up in the Riverton General ER for four hours because of cross‑contact; Culture Night on May 10, 2024, when a parent showed up visibly intoxicated with “adult” horchata in the parking lot and I had to call Daniel from Riverton City Public Works to help calm her and her crying 6‑year‑old; and now this concern that our SEL work might be perceived as religious.

My instinct is that we need clearer language in our event descriptions: mindfulness/yoga sessions are optional, secular, and focused on stress management, not belief systems. Same with food and behavior expectations – no alcohol on school grounds, any food sold at events must meet basic safety/allergy guidelines.

Would you be willing to help me draft a one‑page “Family Event Wellness & Inclusion Guidelines” sheet we can share with PTA and post on the website? I’d love your SEL lens on how to phrase things in a way that’s firm but inviting.

Thanks for always being a sounding board.

Sarah

metadata.subject: Family Wellness Night – mindfulness feedback and next steps
